metadata.title: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = resonant transmission / transparent barrier; CRIMSON = reflected wave / partial bounce
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no full above-barrier transmission formula, no Fabry-Perot phase algebra, no resonant-tunneling-diode engineering.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Tune a particle's energy just right and a solid barrier becomes perfectly transparent — the particle sails through as if it weren't there. Change the energy slightly and it bounces back.

card.copy: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Tune a particle's energy just right and a solid barrier becomes perfectly transparent — the particle sails through as if it weren't t

beats.narration_text: A standard barrier: some wave tunnels through, some reflects. The transmission never reaches one hundred percent. The reflected wave is always there.
At a special energy, the barrier becomes perfectly transparent. Not ninety-nine percent — exactly one hundred percent passes through. The reflected wave vanishes completely. Why?

card.copy: Normal barrier: always some reflection. At one special energy: zero reflection, perfect transmission. How does a solid barrier turn invisible?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: At a special energy, the barrier becomes perfectly transparent. Not ninety-nine percent — exactly one hundred percent passes through. The reflected wave vanishe

beats.narration_text: Inside the barrier, the wave has a shorter wavelength — the particle has more kinetic energy because of the well depth. The wave bounces back and forth from the two walls.
The front wall reflects some wave back. The back wall also reflects some wave back. When those two reflected waves travel back to the front, they can interfere with each other.

When the barrier width is exactly a whole number of half-wavelengths, the two reflected waves are exactly out of phase with each other — they cancel by destructive interference. The reflected wave vanishes.

This is the same trick as an anti-reflection coating on a camera lens — a thin film of just the right thickness makes the two reflected beams cancel. The physics is identical, only the wavelength scale differs.

Resonance condition: two k-two times L equals n times pi. The wavelength inside times the width equals an integer number of half-wavelengths. Satisfied — zero reflection. Off by even a little — reflection jumps back.

Illustrative example: a barrier of width 0.3 nanometers and depth 5 eV. An electron at about 3 eV kinetic energy hits the first resonance — the wavelength inside is 0.6 nanometers, the barrier is exactly one half-wavelength wide. Perfect transmission.
The barrier doesn't disappear. It fights itself. At the right width and energy, the reflections from both walls cancel each other — and everything gets through.

card.copy: The barrier fights itself. Reflections from both walls cancel. At the right energy, everything gets through.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The barrier doesn't disappear. It fights itself. At the right width and energy, the reflections from both walls cancel each other — and everything gets through.
